contexts.text: In this lecture we are going to study a very simple class of stochastic models called AR(1) processes.
We are going to study AR(1) processes partly because they are useful and partly because they help us understand important concepts.

Formally, a marginal distribution  on  is called **stationary** for  if . (This is the same notion of stationarity that we learned about in the {doc}`lecture on AR(1) processes ` applied to a different setting.) From this equality, we immediately get  for all .
In earlier lectures, such as the one on {doc}`AR(1) processes `, we introduced the notion of a stationary distribution.

Let's write those equations in Python Let's start with a simple example comparing two univariate AR(1) processes with , , and , Let's generate 100 paths of length 200 from model  and compute the likelihood ratio processes As we expected, the likelihood ratio processes goes to  as  increases, indicating that model  is chosen correctly by our algorithm.
Asymptotic stationarity and ergodicity are key assumptions needed to make it possible to learn by applying statistical methods.
For example, we have the following result This situation is often referred to as **asymptotic stationarity** or **global stability**.
The following figure illustrates This example helps to emphasize that asymptotic stationarity is about the distribution, while ergodicity is about the sample path.
Consider the simple threshold autoregressive model This is one of those rare nonlinear stochastic models where an analytical expression for the stationary density is available.
Consider the threshold autoregressive model in {eq}`statd_tar`.

Let's start with some imports: The **AR(1) model** (autoregressive model of order 1) takes the form where  are scalar-valued parameters (Equation {eq}`can_ar1` is sometimes called a **stochastic difference equation**.) The specification {eq}`can_ar1` generates a time series  as soon as we specify an initial condition .
The system in question is the univariate autoregressive model {eq}`eq_ar_rep`.

The lag operator  and the inverse lag operator   each map an infinite sequence into an infinite sequence according to the transformation rules A **Wold moving average representation**  for  is Applying the inverse of the operator on the right side and using gives the **vector autoregressive representation** **Definition:** A household service technology is said to be **canonical** if -  is nonsingular, and - the absolute values of the eigenvalues of  are strictly less than . **Key invertibility property:** A canonical household service technology maps a service process  in into a corresponding consumption process  for which the implied household capital stock process  is also in .

The error vector in the vector autoregression is the innovation to  and can be taken to be the white noise in a Wold moving average representation, which can be obtained by "inverting" the autoregressive representation.
The particular method that will be used to construct the vector autoregressive representation also proves useful as an intermediate step in constructing a model of an optimal reporting agency.
A suitable bandwidth is not too smooth (underfitting) or too wiggly (overfitting).
The smoothness of the KDE is dependent on how we choose the bandwidth.
When we use a larger bandwidth, the KDE is smoother.
The covariance estimator therefore requires no kernel or bandwidth choice.
The **variance** of  is defined as Variance is also called the *second central moment* of the distribution.
One good option is simulation as before, combined with the empirical distribution function.

To accomplish that for situations in which we are uncertain about parameter values, we shall extend Wecker's {cite}`wecker1979predicting` approach in the following way. - first simulate an initial path of length ; - for a given prior, draw a sample of size  from the posterior joint distribution of parameters  after observing the initial path; - for each draw , simulate a "future path" of length  with parameters  and compute our three "sample path statistics"; - finally, plot the desired statistics from the  samples as an empirical distribution.

Given a sample of  observations , the **empirical distribution** is \delta(x - x_i) = \begin{cases} +\infty & \text{if } x = x_i \\ 0 & \text{if } x \neq x_i \end{cases} Using the mathematics of the Dirac delta function, it follows that \ell(\theta; X) = \sum_{i=1}^n \log p_\theta(x_i) , Thus, MLE is equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence from the empirical distribution to the statistical model .
With an empirical distribution of likelihood ratios in hand, we can draw **receiver operating characteristic curves** by enumerating pairs given each sample size .

To appreciate how statisticians connect probabilities to data, the key is to understand the following concepts: * A single draw from a probability distribution * Repeated independently  and identically distributed (i.i.d.)  draws of "samples" or "realizations" from the same probability distribution * A **statistic** defined as a  function of a sequence of samples * An **empirical distribution** or **histogram** (a binned empirical distribution) that records observed  **relative frequencies** * The idea that a  population probability  distribution is  what we anticipate **relative frequencies** will be in a long sequence of i.i.d. draws.
That is, The **mean** or **expected value** of a random variable  with distribution  is Expectation is also called the *first moment* of the distribution.
